feedback_text:
Your draft has a clear central concern: widespread AI use is reshaping coursework in a way that feels unfair to students who do their own work, and you argue that a university-level ban is the right response. That’s a focused, arguable claim, and your personal stake (protecting academic integrity) gives the piece energy. To make the argument stronger on the page, though, you’ll need to move from broad assertions (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) to specific, supportable evidence and clearer definitions. What exactly counts as “AI” in your argument (grammar tools, tutoring chatbots, code assistants, image generators)? What does a “ban” look like in practice—ban on generating full responses, ban on any AI-assisted drafting, ban only during exams? Right now, the claim is strong but the boundaries are fuzzy, which leaves readers room to dismiss your position as unrealistic rather than engaging with it.

Your tone is passionate and urgent, which can be effective, but it sometimes slips into language that may alienate readers you need to persuade (especially classmates and instructors). Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous” and “AI slop” communicate frustration, but they also sound dismissive and can make the piece feel like a vent rather than a reasoned argument. Consider revising toward a more precise, academic voice: describe the problem (unequal effort, grade distortions, weakened learning outcomes) in concrete terms, and let your examples and reasoning carry the force. Also, be careful with nostalgia (“back in the day…”) and absolute predictions (“creativity dies”), which can read as overgeneralizations; you can keep the concern but frame it as a risk you can explain and document rather than a guaranteed outcome.

For improvement, focus on structure, evidence, and revision at the sentence level. Structurally, try: (1) introduce the problem and define your terms, (2) present 2–3 reasons a ban (or a narrower policy) is necessary, each with an example or source, (3) address a counterargument (e.g., AI as accessibility support, tutoring, language help, or career preparation), and (4) propose a practical policy with enforcement and equity in mind. Evidence could include your institution’s academic integrity policy, a short quotation from a syllabus, research on AI and learning, or a specific scenario showing how AI shifts grading. Finally, you have several run-on sentences and agreement issues (“commonplace,” “an everyday,” “appliances”), so breaking long sentences into shorter ones and proofreading for clarity will make your writing sharper and more persuasive.

Your draft has a clear, passionate central claim: you support banning AI at the university level because you believe it undermines learning, fairness, and creativity. That conviction comes through strongly, and the reader can tell you’re writing from direct experience. Where your argument needs strengthening is in how you move from personal frustration (“every student in my class uses AI”) to a broad policy conclusion (a university-wide ban). Right now, the piece leans on sweeping statements and predictions (“we are all regurgitating AI slop,” “creativity dies”) without enough concrete support. To make your claim persuasive to readers who don’t already agree, you’ll want to define key terms (what counts as “AI use” vs. “cheating”?), clarify the scope (ban AI in all contexts or only for graded work?), and add evidence beyond observation—examples of specific classroom situations, references to academic integrity policies, or research on learning outcomes and AI-assisted writing.

Your tone is intense and urgent, which can be effective, but at points it becomes more accusatory than argumentative. Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous” and “AI slop” communicate anger, yet they can also push skeptical readers away or make your stance feel reactive rather than reasoned. Consider shifting from condemnation to credibility: acknowledge why students use AI (time pressure, unclear expectations, tutoring gaps), concede limited benefits (accessibility, brainstorming, feedback), and then explain why those benefits still don’t justify unrestricted use in graded work. If you want to critique faculty AI use, you’ll also need to handle that claim carefully—either provide a specific example or frame it as a concern about transparency and course design rather than an accusation. A more measured tone will help you sound like someone proposing a workable policy, not only venting about an unfair system.

For improvement, focus on structure and specificity. Start with a revised thesis that includes your “because” reasons and your proposed solution (e.g., a ban on AI-generated submission, required disclosure of AI assistance, or AI-free assessments), then organize body paragraphs around separate claims: (1) fairness/assessment validity, (2) learning and skill development, (3) long-term creativity/knowledge concerns. Add one or two concrete examples in each section—what an AI-assisted take-home test looks like, how it changes studying, what you’ve observed about grading outcomes. Finally, your sentences are often very long and run together; break them up to improve clarity and control, and watch agreement issues (“an everyday appliance,” “everyday appliances,” “a” vs. “an”). With clearer definitions, evidence, and a more controlled tone, your piece can move from a strong complaint to a convincing argument.

Your draft has a clear, urgent claim: AI has changed schoolwork in a way that feels unfair to students who do the work themselves, and you argue the university should ban AI use beyond limited “assistive” functions. That’s a strong starting position, and your personal stake (integrity, effort, grading equity) gives the piece energy. To make the argument convincing to readers who don’t already agree with you, though, you’ll need to move from broad statements (“every student in my class uses AI,” “assignments have become trivial”) to specific, supportable evidence and clearer reasoning. What kinds of assignments are being undermined? How do you know AI use is widespread, and how is it affecting outcomes (grades, learning, skills)? Also, your conclusion currently contains a tension: you “support the ban” but also permit AI “as a tool to assist academic endeavors.” Those two ideas can work together, but only if you define where the line is—what counts as “assist,” what counts as doing the work, and how the university could reasonably enforce that boundary.

Your tone is passionate and readable, but at times it slides into language that may alienate your audience or make your claims feel overstated. Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous,” “AI slop,” and “we must immediately stop this” create a moral indictment that can sound more like a rant than an argument, especially for academic readers who expect measured claims and careful qualifiers. You can keep the urgency while tightening the rhetoric: focus on fairness, learning outcomes, and academic standards rather than on calling other students lazy or implying faculty are careless. Consider reframing some lines to emphasize systems over individuals (e.g., “current policies and assignment designs make it easy to substitute AI output for learning”), and incorporate at least one fair counterargument—accessibility benefits, language support, tutoring-like uses, workplace relevance—then explain why your policy still makes sense despite those benefits.

For revision, start by giving yourself a sharper thesis and roadmap early: one sentence that states what you want universities to do, why, and on what grounds (fairness, skill development, credibility of grades). Then organize the body into distinct points with concrete examples (a take-home exam where AI can generate full answers; a writing assignment where originality and voice matter; a problem set where step-by-step reasoning is the learning). You’ll also want to define key terms: “AI” (ChatGPT? Grammarly? calculators?), “ban” (campus-wide prohibition or assignment-by-assignment?), and “assist” (brainstorming, outlining, grammar checks, citing sources?)—and propose a workable policy (e.g., disclosure requirements, AI-free assessments, process-based grading, or limited approved tools). Finally, spend time polishing sentence structure and grammar: many sentences run on with multiple clauses, which dilutes your point. Breaking them into shorter, precise sentences will make your argument feel more controlled, credible, and persuasive.

You’ve got a clear central claim—AI use in university should be banned beyond limited “assistance”—and you ground it in a real problem: fairness and the erosion of learning when students outsource thinking. The draft’s energy helps communicate urgency, but your argument currently leans heavily on personal experience and sweeping statements (“every student,” “even some faculty”) without evidence. To make the claim persuasive to readers who don’t already agree with you, tighten the thesis into a specific, arguable policy position (what exactly should be banned, in which contexts, and why), and then support it with concrete reasons and examples. For instance, you could separate your reasons into a few distinct claims—(1) academic integrity/fairness, (2) loss of skill development, (3) devaluation of assessment—and then explain how each connects to your conclusion.

Your tone is passionate and morally engaged, which can be effective, but right now it sometimes shifts into exaggeration and resentment (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous,” “creativity dies”). That language risks pushing neutral readers away or making your argument sound more like a vent than a proposal. Consider revising toward a firm but measured tone: describe the problem, the stakes, and the consequences with precision rather than intensity. Also watch the way you portray other students and faculty—accusations without proof can undermine your credibility. A stronger rhetorical move is to acknowledge that some AI use can be legitimate (brainstorming, accessibility, tutoring, proofreading) and then argue why those uses should be clearly disclosed or limited, while replacing “ban everything” with a policy that targets the specific harms you identify.

For improvement, focus on clarity, structure, and definition. Right now the piece is one long paragraph with several run-on sentences, which makes your ideas harder to follow; break it into paragraphs with topic sentences and revise for sentence boundaries. Define key terms early: what counts as “AI” (ChatGPT? Grammarly? calculators?), what counts as “assist,” and what counts as unacceptable use. Add at least one counterargument and respond to it (e.g., “AI is inevitable; banning it is unenforceable,” or “AI can help learning when used transparently”), then explain why your approach is still better. Finally, consider offering a realistic alternative to a total ban—such as AI-use disclosure requirements, redesigned assessments (in-class writing, process drafts, oral defenses), and explicit guidelines—so your conclusion feels actionable rather than purely alarmed.

Your central argument is clear: you believe AI has shifted schoolwork from learning to outsourcing, and that this undermines fairness, effort, and creativity—so you support a university-level ban (with limited “assistive” use). The draft has energy and a strong sense of stakes, and your best moves are the concrete comparisons (AI as an “appliance,” “take-home tests redundant,” “regurgitating AI”) that help readers visualize what you’re worried about. To strengthen the argument, tighten your claim into a more precise thesis with defined boundaries: *What exactly should be banned (ChatGPT-style text generation? AI math solvers? grammar checkers? coding assistants?) and in which contexts (take-home exams, essays, problem sets, discussion posts)?* Right now, the ending says both “ban AI” and “beyond a tool to assist academic endeavors,” which can sound contradictory unless you specify what “assist” means and what crosses the line into substitution.

Your tone is passionate and morally serious, which can be effective, but it sometimes drifts into sweeping statements that a skeptical academic reader will push back on (“Every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty,” “AI slop,” “creativity dies”). Those lines communicate frustration, but they also risk making your stance seem more emotional than evidence-based. Consider replacing absolutes with more careful phrasing (“many students,” “I’ve seen classmates,” “it can encourage low-effort responses”) and adding a moment of acknowledgement that readers may value AI for legitimate reasons (accessibility, tutoring, brainstorming, translation, learning support). Addressing that counterargument directly—and then explaining why you still think a ban is the best policy—will make you sound fair-minded and harder to dismiss.

For revision, focus on organization and support. Try a structure like: (1) define the problem (what you’re seeing and why it matters), (2) explain harms (fairness/learning/assessment validity), (3) propose a specific policy (what’s banned vs. allowed, how it’s enforced, and what alternatives instructors should use), and (4) conclude with the bigger stakes. Add at least one or two concrete examples or sources—campus policy debates, studies on learning outcomes, or even a short anecdote with specific details—so the essay relies less on generalizations. On the sentence level, you’ll also want to break up run-on sentences and fix a few consistency issues (“commonplace,” “an everyday appliance,” “unlike everyday appliances,” “a tool…not our arms and legs”). Your ideas are strong; the next step is to make the claims narrower, better supported, and more academically controlled so your audience follows you all the way to the policy conclusion.

Your draft has a clear central claim—AI use should be banned at the university level—and you build urgency by linking AI to fairness (“someone who hasn’t put in half the effort…scores above me”) and to long-term consequences (loss of creativity, replacement of learning with “prompt engineering”). That said, the argument currently relies on broad statements (“Every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) rather than verifiable evidence. To make your claim persuasive to readers who aren’t already on your side, you’ll need to define key terms (what counts as “AI”? grammar checkers? tutoring-style chatbots? citation tools?) and narrow the scope of your ban (in which courses, assignments, or contexts?). Consider adding specific examples (a particular type of assignment that becomes meaningless with AI; a concrete scenario where integrity is undermined) and at least one source or data point (university policy, a study on learning outcomes, an academic integrity report). Right now, the draft has strong conviction, but it needs clearer boundaries and support so it reads as an argument—not just a justified frustration.

Your tone is passionate and, at times, accusatory, which creates energy but can also make readers defensive. Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous” and “AI slop” communicate how fed up you are, but they risk undercutting your credibility with an academic audience—especially when you’re asking for a policy-level response. You can keep your intensity while shifting toward a more measured, analytical voice: name the problem, explain the stakes, and show why your solution follows. Also, watch for nostalgia-based framing (“back in the day…”) because it can come across as moralizing rather than reasoning; instead, focus on what educational goals you believe assignments should measure (practice, mastery, critical thinking, original synthesis) and how AI changes that measurement.

For revision, I’d recommend reorganizing into a more deliberate structure: (1) introduction with a precise thesis and definitions; (2) body paragraphs that each make one claim with evidence (fairness/assessment validity; learning and skill development; institutional responsibility); (3) a paragraph that addresses a counterargument (e.g., AI as accessibility support, workplace relevance, or equity) and explains why your policy still makes sense or how you’d accommodate exceptions; (4) a conclusion that proposes a realistic solution. Even if you still argue for a ban, you’ll strengthen your credibility by proposing enforceable guidelines (in-class writing, oral defenses, process drafts, disclosure requirements) and by acknowledging gray areas instead of treating all AI use as identical. Finally, do a sentence-level edit for clarity and precision—several sentences run on, and tightening them will make your reasoning sharper and harder to dismiss.

Your draft has a clear, urgent central claim: AI use has made academic work feel unfair and hollow, and you ultimately argue for a university-level ban. The strongest part of your argument is the ethical tension you name—students who do the work “manually” can be disadvantaged compared to students who outsource thinking to a tool. Right now, though, the essay leans heavily on personal experience and broad statements (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) without evidence or specific examples. To persuade readers beyond those who already agree with you, you’ll want to define your key terms (what counts as “AI use”? brainstorming? grammar checking? generating full answers?) and narrow the scope: are you arguing for a ban on submitting AI-generated work, a ban on AI tools during assessments, or a full prohibition of AI-assisted writing? Those are different policies with different justifications, and clarifying that will make your claim more precise and defensible.

Your tone is passionate and readable, and your frustration comes through clearly—which can be a strength in an argument about academic integrity. At the same time, some phrasing (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous”) risks sounding more like a rant than a reasoned case, especially for an academic audience that may include instructors who use AI responsibly or students who rely on accessibility tools. You can keep the urgency while building credibility by balancing strong language with measured reasoning: acknowledge why AI appeals to students (time pressure, workload, anxiety, unclear expectations), and then explain why those reasons still don’t justify certain uses. Also consider including a brief counterargument paragraph (“Some argue AI can support learning by…”) and then respond to it—this will strengthen your ethos and show you’re thinking critically rather than only reacting.

For improvement, focus on structure and support. Break up the long opening sentence and several run-ons into shorter, controlled sentences so your points land cleanly. Consider organizing the essay into: (1) the problem (what’s happening and why it matters), (2) the harms (fairness, learning loss, creativity, trust), (3) the solution (what policy you propose), and (4) implementation (how it would work and what exceptions exist—e.g., disability accommodations, citation/transparent use, or discipline-specific tools). If you still want to argue for a ban, you’ll need to address feasibility and enforcement: how would instructors detect violations, and what prevents the policy from punishing honest students? Alternatively, you might find your argument becomes even stronger if you advocate for a targeted policy (clear rules + transparent attribution + assessment redesign) rather than a total ban. Either way, adding concrete examples, clearer definitions, and a more structured progression will turn your strong feelings into a persuasive academic argument.

You have a clear, forceful central claim: AI use in college has undermined meaningful learning and fair evaluation, so you support banning it at the university level (with limited “tool” exceptions). That’s an arguable thesis with real stakes, and your draft conveys why you care—academic integrity, effort, and the purpose of assignments. To strengthen the argument, though, you’ll need to sharpen what you mean by “AI” (spellcheck? Grammarly? ChatGPT-style text generation? code assistants?) and what you mean by a “ban” (in all courses, or only for graded work, or only for certain tasks?). Right now, you argue both “AI should be treated as a tool” and “ban AI at university level,” which can sound contradictory unless you define exactly what uses you would still permit and why.

Your tone is passionate and urgent, which can be effective, but it sometimes leans into sweeping claims and loaded language that may make skeptical readers tune out. Phrases like “Every student in my class uses AI,” “AI slop,” and “back in the day” are emotionally vivid, but they read as overgeneralizations or nostalgia rather than evidence-based reasoning. If your goal is to persuade an academic audience (faculty, administrators, or classmates), aim for a steadier, more precise tone: replace absolute statements with qualified ones (“many students,” “in my experience,” “in several courses”) and focus on describing harms in concrete terms (grade inflation, reduced skill development, difficulty assessing authorship) rather than insulting the output or the people using it.

For improvement, focus on structure and support. Consider organizing the essay around 2–3 specific harms (fairness/grades, learning outcomes, assignment design) and, for each, provide an example from your experience plus a broader reason it matters. Then include at least one counterargument—e.g., AI as an accessibility support, a brainstorming tutor, or a way to learn faster—and explain why those benefits don’t outweigh the costs *or* how your policy could preserve benefits while preventing abuse. Finally, revise for clarity and sentence control: several sentences run long and rely on commas where you need periods; watch article/number agreement (“an everyday appliance,” “appliances”), and tighten phrasing so your strongest points don’t get buried. If you revise with clearer definitions, a more measured tone, and specific evidence, your argument will feel much harder to dismiss.

Your draft makes a clear, passionate argument: you believe widespread AI use is undermining learning, fairness, and creativity, and you ultimately call for a university-level ban. The urgency comes through, and you do a good job explaining the “why” behind your stance—especially the idea that when AI replaces the struggle of working through a problem, students lose the chance to actually learn. Right now, though, your argument leans heavily on broad claims (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) without evidence, which makes your position easier to dismiss. Consider strengthening your credibility by defining what counts as “AI use” (spellcheck? Grammarly? ChatGPT for outlining? generating full answers?) and then supporting your claims with concrete examples: a specific type of assignment where AI makes the task “trivial,” a pattern you’ve observed, or even a brief reference to a policy, study, or survey about student AI use. Also, your thesis at the end is a bit split: you say AI is a tool that can assist academic endeavors, but you also “fully support the ban.” You’ll want to clarify what exactly you’re banning and what you still allow.

Your tone is forceful and morally charged, which can be effective, but some phrases (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous”) risk sounding more like a rant than an academic argument. If your audience includes instructors or administrators—people you’re trying to persuade—dialing back the heat and increasing precision will make your message stronger. You can still communicate frustration while using language that signals fairness and seriousness (e.g., “This creates an uneven playing field,” “This weakens skill development,” “This pressures students to compromise integrity”). It would also help to acknowledge a reasonable counterargument: some students use AI as accessibility support (language barriers, disability accommodations, brainstorming), and some disciplines may legitimately integrate AI as a professional tool. If you address that honestly and then explain why your policy proposal still stands (or how it could make exceptions), you’ll come across as more credible and thoughtful.

For revision, focus on structure and specificity. Break up long sentences (your opening sentence especially) and organize the essay into clear moves: (1) the problem (what AI use is doing to learning and fairness), (2) the consequences (skills, integrity, classroom trust), (3) the solution (what policy you want and how it would work). Right now, “ban AI” is too broad to be workable—propose something enforceable, like banning undisclosed AI-generated content on graded work, requiring disclosure statements, redesigning assessments (in-class writing, process drafts, oral reflections), or allowing limited AI use for brainstorming with citation. Finally, add a few concrete examples of assignments and what “academic integrity” looks like in those cases—show me the line you want the university to draw, and why that line is the most fair.

Your draft has a clear central claim—AI use in university should be banned beyond limited “assisting” functions—and your strongest asset is the urgency you bring to the topic. Right now, though, the argument leans heavily on generalizations (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty”) and personal frustration as proof. To make your position persuasive to readers who don’t already agree with you, you’ll want to narrow and define what you mean by “AI” (ChatGPT-style text generation? grammar checkers? calculators? coding assistants?) and what “ban” would actually cover. As written, your thesis risks sounding absolute while also leaving wiggle room (“beyond a tool to assist”)—so consider revising to a more precise claim like: *Universities should prohibit undisclosed AI-generated content in graded work, require transparency, and redesign assessments to prioritize process and original thinking.* Precision will make your stance harder to dismiss.

Your tone is passionate and morally serious, which fits a piece about academic integrity, but it sometimes shifts into language that may alienate your audience (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous,” “regurgitating”). If your goal is to convince classmates or faculty, you’ll be more effective if you keep the intensity while reducing insults and mind-reading about others’ motives. Try framing concerns as systems and incentives rather than as bad individuals: instead of “someone who hasn’t put in half the effort,” you could say that AI changes the relationship between effort and outcomes and can undermine fair evaluation. Also, “back in the day” is vague; if you want to contrast past and present, specify what changed (assessment design, access to information, expectations about drafts, collaboration, etc.) and avoid sounding nostalgic as your main evidence.

For improvement, focus on structure and support. Build a roadmap: (1) define the problem (what counts as misuse), (2) explain harms (fairness, learning, skill development, trust), (3) address counterarguments (accessibility benefits, tutoring, brainstorming, ESL support, workplace relevance), and (4) propose workable solutions (disclosure policies, process-based grading, in-class writing, version history, oral check-ins). Right now you move quickly from personal experience to broad predictions (“creativity dies”), which would be stronger if you grounded it in specific examples or credible sources (university policies, studies on learning, academic integrity research). Finally, edit for clarity and control: several sentences are run-ons and could be broken into shorter units to sharpen your logic and make your strongest points land.

Your draft makes a clear, urgent claim: AI use in college has made learning and assessment unfair, and you ultimately argue for a university-level ban (with limited “tool” exceptions). The strongest part of your argument is the ethical appeal—you position yourself as someone trying to maintain academic integrity while the system rewards shortcuts, and that’s a compelling motive for readers to take seriously. Right now, though, the reasoning leans heavily on broad statements (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty”), which makes your case easier to dismiss as exaggeration. To strengthen your argument, define what counts as “AI use” (spellcheck? tutoring-style feedback? full answer generation?) and be specific about the harms you’re describing (grade inflation, reduced learning, inequitable access, unreliable assessment). Consider adding at least one concrete example (a type of assignment where AI makes cheating easy) and at least one credible source (a university policy, a study on learning outcomes, or reporting on AI detection limits) so the argument stands on more than personal frustration.

Your tone is passionate and readable, which can be a real asset—but it sometimes shifts into venting, and that can undercut your credibility with an academic audience. Phrases like “AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous,” and “regurgitating” communicate intensity, but they also risk sounding dismissive of classmates and faculty, which may make resistant readers stop listening. You can keep your urgency while adopting a more controlled, persuasive tone: describe the problem in measured language, acknowledge why students are tempted to use AI (workload, pressure, unclear rules), and then explain why that doesn’t justify it. The line “back in the day” also reads as a rhetorical flourish rather than evidence; if you want a “then vs. now” contrast, ground it in what has actually changed in assessment design and student behavior rather than nostalgia.

For revision, focus on sharpening your thesis into a policy claim you can defend: what exactly should be banned, in what contexts, and how would it be enforced without causing new problems (false accusations, accessibility issues, unequal enforcement)? Right now you say AI should be “a tool” but also support a “ban,” so the essay needs a clearer boundary between acceptable assistance and prohibited substitution—otherwise readers will see a contradiction. Build the body around 2–3 main points (fairness, learning/skill development, integrity of assessment), and for each one: make a claim, provide evidence or an example, and explain why it leads logically to your proposed ban. Finally, as you revise, break up the long sentences and reduce comma splices—cleaner sentence structure will make your argument feel more deliberate and harder to dismiss.

Your draft has a clear central claim—AI use has made coursework feel unfair and hollow, and you ultimately argue for a university-level ban—but right now the argument relies more on intensity than on support. You make several big assertions (“Every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty are simply using AI to generate coursework”) that readers will question unless you define what you mean and provide evidence (specific examples, observations, or credible sources). Consider tightening your thesis into one or two sentences that state *exactly* what you want banned (all generative AI? for graded work only? in certain courses?) and why (fairness, learning outcomes, academic integrity). From there, build a sequence of reasons with concrete proof: what harms you’ve seen, what learning is lost, and what measurable outcomes you predict if AI remains unrestricted.

Your tone is passionate and urgent, which can be effective, but at times it slips into sweeping generalizations and loaded phrasing (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous”) that may push skeptical readers away. Since your audience in a college argument essay is likely mixed—including students who use AI and faculty who may allow limited use—you’ll persuade more effectively if you adopt a controlled, specific tone: describe the problem precisely, acknowledge why people use AI (time pressure, unclear assignments, accessibility), and then explain why those reasons still don’t justify certain uses. Adding a brief counterargument paragraph would strengthen your credibility: for instance, some argue AI can support brainstorming, tutoring, or accessibility; you can concede limited benefits while still arguing that using AI to produce graded answers undermines learning and fairness.

For revision, focus on structure and clarity. Break up the long opening sentence and clean up grammar (“commonplace,” “an everyday appliance,” “everyday appliances,” “has become,” “which are specialized,” etc.) so your points land more sharply. Then organize the essay into clear paragraphs: (1) define the problem and your thesis/policy proposal; (2) explain harms with examples (grades, skill-building, academic integrity), possibly including a brief counterargument; (3) propose a realistic solution (what a “ban” looks like, enforcement, acceptable vs. unacceptable uses, and what instructors should do instead—more in-class writing, process drafts, oral check-ins, or transparent AI guidelines). Right now you end by saying AI should be “a tool,” but you also “support the ban”; reconcile that tension by specifying the narrow “assist” uses you would permit and the “do it for me” uses you would prohibit.

Your draft has a clear central claim—AI use in college has gone too far and you support a university-level ban—and your frustration gives the piece urgency. The strongest part of your argument is the fairness concern: you’re pointing to a real problem of uneven effort and unclear standards. Right now, though, the reasoning relies heavily on broad statements (“Every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) and on slippery-slope predictions (“creativity dies,” “we are all regurgitating AI slop”). To make your argument persuasive to a skeptical reader, you’ll need to narrow and define your terms (What counts as “AI”? Spell-check? Grammarly? brainstorming? coding help? full essay generation?), specify what “ban” means (on assignments only? exams? in-class? all coursework?), and add evidence beyond personal observation—class policy examples, a short anecdote with concrete details, or a credible source about learning outcomes and academic integrity.

Your tone is passionate and vivid, which can work well in an opinion piece, but it sometimes turns accusatory or absolute in a way that may push readers away. Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous,” “AI slop,” and the assumption that others “haven’t put in half the effort” communicate anger more than analysis, and they risk sounding like you’re attacking classmates rather than critiquing a system. Consider shifting from blame to stakes: emphasize how unclear AI norms affect learning, assessment validity, and equity. You can keep your voice while making it more rhetorically effective by using more measured wording (e.g., “undermines,” “incentivizes shortcuts,” “makes evaluation unreliable”) and by acknowledging that some students may use AI in limited, legitimate ways—then explain why those uses still need clear boundaries.

For revision, focus on structure and precision. Start with a sharper thesis that includes your proposed policy (for example: “Universities should prohibit uncredited AI-generated work on graded assignments and require transparent disclosure of AI assistance”). Then organize the body into 2–3 reasons (fairness, learning/skill development, assessment integrity), and for each reason provide a concrete example and explain the impact. Add at least one counterargument (accessibility benefits, tutoring support, language assistance, efficiency) and respond to it; this will make you sound fair-minded and strengthen your credibility. Finally, clean up style issues—several run-on sentences and comma splices blur your points—by breaking long sentences into shorter ones and tightening wording (e.g., “commonplace,” “an everyday appliance,” “everyday appliances…specialized”). If you revise with clearer definitions, evidence, and a more controlled tone, your position will read as principled rather than purely reactive.

Your draft has a clear, urgent claim: AI use in university coursework is undermining learning and fairness, so you support a ban. The strongest part of your argument is the connection you draw between AI and academic integrity—especially the frustration of unequal effort leading to unequal outcomes. Right now, though, the argument leans heavily on broad statements (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) and predictions (“creativity dies,” “AI slop”) without evidence. To make your position persuasive to readers who don’t already agree with you, tighten your thesis into something specific and arguable (for example: *universities should prohibit AI-generated submission on graded work and restrict AI to clearly defined support uses*), and then build a few focused reasons that you can support with examples, course policies, or research on learning outcomes.

Your tone is passionate and vivid, which gives the piece energy, but it also slips into bitterness and absolutes that may push readers away. Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous” and “AI slop” communicate how you feel, but they can make your stance sound more like a rant than a college argument. You’ll come across as more credible if you keep the urgency while dialing back the sweeping judgments and framing your frustration as a shared problem: a system that incentivizes shortcuts. Also, be careful with the nostalgia move (“back in the day…”). It can be effective, but as written it reads more like a moral contrast than a reasoned comparison. If you keep it, ground it in what those older methods did pedagogically (practice, feedback, struggle, retention) and explain what AI changes about that learning process.

For revision, focus on clarity and precision. First, define what you mean by “AI” and what exactly you want banned: using AI to *generate* answers? to *edit* writing? to *tutor*? Also watch for overgeneralizations like “every student in my class uses AI”; if you can’t substantiate that, reframe it (“many,” “I often see,” “it feels common”) and then add evidence.

Your tone is passionate and engaging, but it sometimes slips into language that may make skeptical readers shut down (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous,” “regurgitating”). In academic argument, you’ll persuade more effectively if you keep the moral seriousness while reducing the heat: describe the consequences precisely rather than relying on ridicule or worst-case projections. You can still convey frustration, but aim for a tone of reasoned concern: explain what you’ve observed, why it matters, and what principle is at stake (fair assessment, authentic learning, equity). Also, be careful with the “back in the day” contrast—readers may challenge it as nostalgia unless you connect it to a clear learning principle (productive struggle, practice, feedback loops) and show how AI can short-circuit that process.

For revision, I’d like you to define your key terms and make your proposed policy workable. What counts as “AI,” exactly (grammar checkers, autocomplete, tutoring bots, citation generators)? What counts as “assist” versus “doing the work,” and how would instructors enforce that consistently without intrusive surveillance? You’ll also strengthen your credibility by acknowledging a counterargument: some students use AI as a tutor, for accessibility, or to brainstorm responsibly—so explain why a ban is better than alternatives (AI literacy instruction, redesigned assessments, disclosure requirements, in-class writing, oral defenses, process drafts). Finally, consider reorganizing: start with a focused thesis, then body paragraphs that each make one claim with evidence, then a conclusion that returns to your core value (learning) and clarifies the exact change you want your university to adopt.

Your draft makes a clear, passionate argument: AI has become so widely used in school that it threatens fairness, learning, and creativity, and you conclude that universities should ban AI beyond limited “assistance.” The core strength here is that you’re writing from a recognizable stake—your commitment to academic integrity—and you connect that stake to specific classroom consequences (homework feels trivial, take-home tests feel redundant, peers “score above” you). To sharpen the argument, though, you’ll want to move from broad claims (“every student in my class uses AI,” “even some faculty…”) to claims you can reasonably support and define. Right now, “AI” covers a lot: grammar tools, search engines with AI, tutoring chatbots, full essay generation, coding helpers, etc. If your thesis is a ban, you’ll need to specify what exactly is being banned, what counts as “assistance,” and why that line is the most ethical and practical one.

Your tone is urgent and morally serious, which can be effective, but some phrasing risks sounding accusatory or absolute in a way that may make skeptical readers tune out. Phrases like “absolutely ridiculous,” “AI slop,” and “we must immediately stop this” heighten the emotion, but they also reduce the sense that you’re weighing complexity—especially since many instructors and institutions are trying to set realistic guidelines rather than simply “allow” cheating. Consider revising toward a more academic tone that still preserves your conviction: state the problem, show concrete impacts, and explain why your proposed solution is better than alternatives. Also, when you reference “back in the day,” be careful: readers may ask when that was and whether it’s an idealized comparison. If you frame the contrast as “when students hit a roadblock, the work of figuring it out produced learning,” you keep the focus on your main value (learning) rather than nostalgia.

For revision, focus on three improvements: (1) tighten your thesis and scope. For example, you might argue for banning *unauthorized generative AI output on graded work* while still allowing limited uses (brainstorming, proofreading, citation help) with disclosure—or, if you truly want a full ban, explain how it would be enforced and why partial policies fail. (2) Add evidence and examples: a specific scenario from your class, a university policy, or a study about learning outcomes when students outsource thinking. Even a brief, concrete anecdote (without naming anyone) will make your fairness argument more credible. (3) Address counterarguments: accessibility (AI as a tutor for first-gen students), workload realities, and the idea that AI literacy is now part of education. You can still disagree, but acknowledging these points and responding to them will make your argument feel stronger and more persuasive. As you revise, also break up a few long sentences and fix small grammar issues (“commonplace,” “an everyday,” “appliances,” subject-verb agreement) so your style matches the seriousness of your claim.

Your draft has a clear central concern—AI use is changing academic work in ways you think are unfair and damaging—and your strongest argumentative move is the ethical appeal to integrity (“someone who hasn’t put in half the effort…scores above me”). That said, right now your claim is broader than what you’ve proven. Lines like “Every student in my class uses AI” and “even some faculty are simply using AI” read as sweeping assertions; if you want readers to accept them, you’ll need either evidence (specific examples, observations, policies you’ve seen, data, or a brief anecdote with concrete detail) or more careful wording (e.g., “many,” “I’ve noticed,” “it sometimes seems”). Your thesis also shifts: you describe AI as an “everyday appliance,” then argue for a full university-level ban, but you also say it can be used “as a tool to assist academic endeavors.” Those positions can clash unless you define exactly what counts as “assist” versus “do it for us,” and what a “ban” would actually ban (ChatGPT for drafting? grammar checking? coding autocomplete? citation managers?). Tightening definitions and scope will make your argument more logically consistent and harder to dismiss.

Your tone is passionate and urgent, which can be persuasive—especially when you describe exhaustion and the pressure to “stay level with their peers.” However, some phrases (“AI slop,” “absolutely ridiculous”) risk sounding more like venting than reasoning, which can cause skeptical readers (including instructors) to tune out. Consider aiming for controlled intensity: keep your moral seriousness, but pair it with a more measured voice and a willingness to distinguish between different uses of AI. One way to strengthen credibility is to acknowledge a counterpoint fairly—e.g., AI can support accessibility, tutoring, language support, or brainstorming—then explain why those benefits still don’t justify certain uses (like producing full answers on take-home exams). When you show you’ve considered the other side, your call for policy change reads less like frustration and more like a thoughtful proposal.

For revision, focus on structure and specificity. Start with a sharper thesis that names your exact claim and the boundaries of it (for example: “Universities should restrict AI use on graded work that measures individual skill, while allowing transparent, limited AI support for practice and accessibility”). Then organize body paragraphs around 2–3 main reasons: fairness/assessment validity, learning/skill development, and institutional responsibility (including faculty use). Add at least one concrete example for each reason (a type of assignment where AI undermines learning; a scenario where classmates gain an unfair edge; a policy you think would work). Finally, edit for sentence control: many sentences run long and stack clauses, which blurs your points. Break them up, watch article/plural consistency (“an everyday appliance,” “everyday appliances”), and replace vague language with precise terms. If you do those things, your argument will move from an emotional complaint to a persuasive, policy-ready position.

Also, your “back in the day” contrast sets up a nostalgia argument; it could be stronger if you framed it as a learning-process argument (struggle, revision, help-seeking, and feedback are part of developing competence) rather than implying the past was automatically better.

For improvement, focus on structure and specificity. Break up the long opening sentence and several run-ons so your key points land with more force; right now the pacing blurs important ideas together. Organize the essay into clear moves: (1) define AI use and the problem you see, (2) explain harms (learning, fairness, creativity) with examples, (3) address counterarguments (accessibility accommodations, brainstorming, tutoring-like support, uneven enforcement), and (4) propose a realistic policy (what’s prohibited, what’s permitted, how students disclose use, and how assignments can be designed to reduce cheating). If you refine your definitions, provide concrete evidence, and temper the tone into firm but fair persuasion, you’ll have a much stronger argument that reads less like a vent and more like a credible academic position.

Your draft has a clear stance—by the end, I understand that you support banning AI at the university level—and you give readers a strong sense of *why*: fairness, learning, and the long-term impact on creativity and effort. To make your position persuasive to an academic audience, you’ll need to narrow and define your key terms (what counts as “AI”? spellcheck? grammar tools? tutoring chatbots? generating full answers?) and then build your case with concrete support: a brief example of how a take-home test became “redundant,” a classroom scenario that shows the grade/effort mismatch, or a credible source about learning outcomes when students outsource thinking. A strong next step would be to revise your thesis into a more precise, arguable policy claim—for example: “Universities should prohibit generative AI from producing graded work, while allowing limited, disclosed uses (brainstorming, outlining, editing) because….”

Instead, you can keep your strong voice while making the claims more precise: “In my school,” “in my experience,” “many of my classes,” etc. Also, consider shifting from “theoretical gibberish” to a phrase that still communicates frustration but stays respectful and credible to an academic audience (e.g., “abstract, test-focused material” or “concepts taught without enough application”). That change would help your reader take your critique seriously rather than reading it as venting.

For improvement, focus on clarity and control at the sentence level. Many of your sentences are long run-ons with multiple ideas, which creates intensity but can blur your meaning; breaking them into shorter sentences will make your argument sharper. Proofread for capitalization (“I”), spelling (“at least”), and consistency (“cpp” vs. “CPP”), and watch punctuation around quotes and commas (you don’t need commas inside quotation marks like “rough”, and you can use em dashes or periods instead of repeated commas). Finally, end with a stronger concluding move: instead of stopping on worry (“still wondering how I will pass midterms”), tell us what you want next—what support, strategy, or shift you’re aiming for (office hours, different study methods, choosing more applied courses, reframing exams as skills practice). That will turn your draft from a description of stress into a focused reflection with direction.

You have a clear central claim: you struggle in exam-heavy, theory-forward environments and you expected college to be more “learn by doing,” but you’re discovering exams and adult responsibilities are still major pressures. That through-line is compelling and relatable, and the contrast between your schooling in India and your expectations at CPP sets up an argument with real stakes. To make it stronger as an argument (not just a vent), sharpen your thesis into one sentence early on that states what you’re trying to prove about your learning style and what you want the reader to understand (or what change you’re advocating for). Then organize the draft around 2–3 key points that support that thesis—for example: (1) how exam-based learning affected your performance and confidence, (2) what “practice-based learning” looks like for you and when you thrive, and (3) how the added layer of adulthood/time management intensifies academic pressure. Right now those points are all present, but they’re blended together in one long movement, which makes the argument feel less controlled than it could be.

Your tone is honest, vivid, and sometimes funny (“pops right out”), which gives your voice energy. At the same time, some lines drift into broad generalizations (“In India, the idea is…”) that could come across as unfair or oversimplified, even if you’re describing your own experience. You can keep the critique while making it more precise and credible by grounding it in your specific context: your school, your program/track, your teachers’ methods, the kinds of exams you faced, and how you responded. Similarly, “theoretical gibberish” communicates frustration, but it also risks sounding dismissive; consider describing what exactly is difficult (abstract concepts, test format, time pressure, memorization demands) so the reader sees the problem clearly rather than just hearing anger. If you want the reader to empathize and take your point seriously, specificity will do more work for you than intensity alone.

For improvement, focus on structure and sentence-level clarity. To make the argument more persuasive, I’d like to see you sharpen it into a specific thesis statement early on (1–2 sentences) and then build the essay in steps: (1) what “rough” looked like in your high school context, (2) what you expected from CPP, (3) what you found instead, and (4) what you want to do about it. Right now, the ideas are all there, but they arrive in one long wave; clearer structure will help readers follow your reasoning and take your conclusion seriously.

Your tone is honest, vivid, and engaging—especially the “overloaded memory” image and the sense of pressure stacking up. That said, some phrases come across as sweeping or overly broad (for example, “In India, the idea is…”). Since you’re drawing from personal experience, it will sound stronger and fairer if you frame those statements as your experience within a particular school, region, or classroom culture rather than as a claim about an entire country. Also, consider your audience: if this is a college essay, you can keep the conversational voice, but tightening some informal phrasing (“theoretical gibberish,” “adulting challenges”) can help you sound reflective rather than purely frustrated. You’re at your best when you move from emotion to insight—when you name what’s happening and what it reveals about how you learn.

For improvement, focus on specificity and revision at the sentence level. Add one or two concrete moments (a particular exam you blanked on, a project where you succeeded, a CPP class where the “learn by doing” promise did or didn’t show up). Those examples will turn your points from general feelings into evidence. Then work on clarity: break up run-on sentences, capitalize “I,” and use periods to create readable units (right now many commas are doing the work of full stops). Define “CPP” the first time you mention it, and end with a forward-looking turn—what strategy will you try, what support might you seek, or what change in mindset are you considering? That kind of ending will transform the piece from a vent into an argument about growth, learning, and adaptation.

Your draft has a clear central claim: the SAT is redundant, inequitable, and functions more as a profit-driven system than a meaningful measure of college readiness. The strongest part of your argument is the fairness critique—especially your point that test prep resources track wealth, so the SAT can end up measuring access rather than ability. Where your argument can get sharper is in how you define and support key claims. For instance, the “redundant” point needs a bit more explanation: *what exactly do transcripts/GPAs already measure that the SAT claims to add, and why do you think that added information isn’t valuable?* Also, you set up a counterargument (“equalized measure”) but you mostly dismiss it rather than engaging it: consider acknowledging the best version of that argument (e.g., grade inflation, uneven school rigor) and then explaining, with reasons and examples, why the SAT still fails to solve those problems or introduces worse ones.

Your tone is passionate and forceful, which can be effective, but right now it sometimes reads more like a rant than an academic argument. Phrases like “money grab,” “monopoly with no accountability,” and “little to no respect” communicate frustration, but they can make readers who disagree shut down rather than listen. Try shifting from attacking institutions to evaluating outcomes and evidence. Instead of “It is an undeniable fact that no student truly likes the SAT,” use a more defensible, research-friendly claim (e.g., “Many students report high stress and low perceived value from standardized tests,” and then cite a source). You can still be critical—just aim for language that sounds measured and credible, because that will make your critique harder to dismiss.

For improvement, focus on structure, evidence, and sentence control. Break the opening into a clear thesis statement early (one sentence that names your position and main reasons), then build body paragraphs around distinct points: (1) redundancy/limited validity, (2) inequity and test-prep access, (3) limited subject coverage and impact on different student strengths, plus a conclusion proposing what should replace it (test-optional? portfolio review? stronger transcript evaluation?). Right now you have several long run-on sentences and comma splices; tightening them will instantly increase clarity and authority. Finally, add at least 2–3 concrete supports—data about score gaps by income, research on predictive validity (SAT vs. high school GPA), or examples of colleges going test-optional and what happened—so your argument rests on more than personal conviction.

You have a clear, forceful central claim: the SAT is redundant, inequitable, and functions more as a profit-driven barrier than a meaningful measure of college readiness. The strongest part of your argument is the fairness critique—your point that the test often measures access to prep resources more than “ability” is a credible line of reasoning, and it sets you up well to challenge the “equal baseline” defense. Where your argument needs strengthening is in precision and support. Right now, you make several sweeping assertions (“undeniable fact,” “no student truly likes,” “monopoly with no accountability”) without evidence, and that makes an audience more likely to push back. Consider narrowing the thesis into 1–2 specific, defensible claims (e.g., the SAT’s predictive value is limited compared to GPA; prep access skews outcomes), then backing each with either a statistic, a study, or at least a concrete example. Also, be careful with the “redundant because grades are verified” point: verification doesn’t automatically mean comparability across schools, so you’ll want to explain more carefully why GPA (or other measures) is still a better indicator than a standardized test.

Your tone is passionate and engaging, but it sometimes becomes dismissive or absolutist in a way that can undercut your credibility with skeptical readers. Phrases like “money grab,” “no respect,” and “anyone can see” signal strong emotion, but they can make the piece feel more like a rant than an argument aimed at persuading a mixed audience (including readers who think standardized tests have some value). You can keep your conviction while sounding more academically persuasive by letting the evidence do the work and by acknowledging complexity: for example, you might concede that standardized tests offer some cross-school comparison, then argue that the costs, bias, and limited predictive value outweigh that benefit. Shifting from “colleges force them to” to a more precise claim—“many selective colleges historically required it, which pressures applicants”—will also help your argument sound fair and accurate.

For improvement, focus on structure and clarity. This draft is essentially one long sentence, which makes it hard for your reader to follow your logic and identify your main reasons. Break it into paragraphs organized by claims (1) what the SAT is supposed to do, (2) why it fails (resource disparity, narrow skill focus), and (3) what should replace it (test-optional, GPA + coursework rigor, portfolios, writing samples, interviews, etc.). Replace informal phrasing (“heck,” “like as if,” “cracked it”) with more formal wording, and watch for grammar/word choice (“in fact,” “high school grades,” “SAT’s removal”). Finally, strengthen your conclusion by moving beyond condemnation of the College Board to a clear policy position: what exactly should colleges do instead, and why would that produce a fairer or better admissions process?

You have a clear central claim: the SAT is redundant, inequitable, and functions more as a profit-driven barrier than a meaningful measure of readiness. That’s a strong starting argument, and you’re already anticipating a common counterclaim (“equalized measure”) and pushing back against it. To make your position more persuasive, though, you’ll need to narrow and clarify your thesis. Right now you’re making several related claims at once—redundant with grades, biased toward wealth, irrelevant to many talents/subjects, and “monopoly with no accountability.” Consider choosing 2–3 key reasons and building the essay around them. Also, be careful with absolute statements like “undeniable fact” and “no student truly likes the SAT”; these are easy for a reader to challenge. If you want to keep those ideas, revise them into defensible claims (e.g., “Many students report…” or “The test is widely unpopular…”) and support them with evidence.

Your tone is passionate, which can work in persuasive writing, but at the moment it reads more like a rant than a college argument because of the sarcasm (“heck give me enough money…”) and highly charged wording (“money grab,” “monopoly,” “little to no respect”). A reader who doesn’t already agree may tune out. You can keep the critical stance while sounding more credible by using more measured language and letting facts do the heavy lifting. For example, instead of “money grab,” you might describe the fee structure, the cost of prep, and how those costs correlate with score gains. Similarly, if you claim the SAT is “redundant,” explain concretely what high school transcripts already measure (GPA across time, course rigor, teacher evaluations) and why those measures might predict college performance as well or better.

For revision, focus on structure and support. Break the draft into paragraphs that each make one main point, and add specific evidence: statistics on score increases tied to income or paid prep, research on whether SAT scores predict college GPA beyond high school grades, or examples of test-optional policies and what happened after adoption. Also, strengthen your counterargument section: acknowledge why colleges like standardized tests (comparability across schools, grade inflation concerns) and then respond with evidence-based rebuttals. Finally, edit for clarity: you have several run-on sentences and phrasing issues (“like as if,” “after the fact that,” “infact”). Shorter sentences, precise transitions, and a clear thesis at the end of the intro will make your argument much easier to follow and much harder to dismiss.

Your draft has a clear central position: you believe the SAT is redundant, inequitable, and primarily benefits the test industry rather than students or colleges. That argument has real potential, especially when you connect test performance to access to resources (tutoring, practice materials, time) rather than to “ability.” Right now, though, your claim reads more like a series of powerful reactions than a structured case. I’d encourage you to sharpen your thesis into one or two precise sentences early on (for example: “The SAT should be removed from admissions because it duplicates information schools already provide and systematically advantages students with greater financial resources”). Then organize your points into distinct chunks—redundancy, inequity, limited predictive value, and curricular mismatch—so each paragraph develops one reason with explanation and support.

Your tone is energetic and confident, which can be a strength, but at the moment it comes across as a rant in places, mainly because of broad statements (“undeniable fact,” “no student truly likes the SAT,” “monopoly with no accountability”) and direct attacks on institutions (“little to no respect”). In academic writing, you’ll persuade more readers if you keep the intensity while grounding it in careful phrasing and evidence. Instead of “undeniable,” try “many students report” or “research suggests,” and consider acknowledging why some colleges still use standardized tests (comparability across schools, grade inflation concerns, differing curricula). Addressing that counterargument directly—then explaining why it’s insufficient—will make your position feel more fair-minded and therefore more convincing.

For revision, focus on development and credibility: add specific evidence (a statistic about score gaps by income, a study on how well SAT scores predict college GPA compared to high school grades, or a credible source on the test-prep industry). Also work on sentence control: you have several long run-on sentences that pile ideas together; breaking them up will make your reasoning easier to follow and will improve your authority as a writer. Finally, define key terms you use (“redundant,” “equalized measure,” “monopoly”) and replace casual phrasing (“heck,” “give me enough money”) with more precise examples (e.g., listing common paid resources and their costs). If you revise with a clearer structure and stronger support, you’ll have a persuasive argument that readers can’t dismiss as just frustration.

Your central argument is clear and compelling: you’re claiming the SAT is redundant (because grades already indicate performance), inequitable (because money buys prep advantages), and therefore not a meaningful measure of college readiness. That’s a strong thesis direction, and you also anticipate a common counterargument (“it equalizes measurement”), which is a smart move. Where your argument needs more development is in how you *prove* each claim. Right now, you assert that high school grades are already “verified,” that the SAT is a “money grab,” and that it’s “undeniable” no students like it—but those are big claims that will read stronger if you back them with specific evidence: data on score gaps by income, research on how well SAT scores predict college GPA compared to high school GPA, and concrete examples of costs (registration, retakes, tutoring, practice materials). Also consider sharpening your line of reasoning: is the SAT redundant because grades are already standardized enough, or is it *not* redundant but still harmful because it amplifies inequality? Pick the core logic and build your paragraphs around it.

Your tone is passionate and urgent, which can work if you channel it into a focused, persuasive academic voice. As it stands, the opening (“every student’s nuisance and annoyance”) and phrases like “heck give me enough money” and “little to no respect” make the piece feel more like a rant than an argument aimed at convincing skeptical readers (like admissions officers, policymakers, or peers who think standardized testing has benefits). You don’t have to become bland, but you do need to sound fair and credible. One way to do that is to replace absolute or sweeping statements (“undeniable,” “no student truly likes”) with more precise phrasing (“many students,” “research suggests,” “in my experience”), and to acknowledge what supporters think the SAT does well before you explain why that benefit is limited or outweighed.

For revision, focus on structure, clarity, and specificity. Break this into multiple paragraphs with a clear thesis near the beginning, then organize body paragraphs by claim (1: redundancy/predictive value, 2: equity/resource gap, 3: limited measurement/subject bias + what should replace it). Add at least one credible source per major point. You’ll also want to edit for sentence boundaries and readability—your draft is mostly one long sentence with commas, which blurs your ideas. Aim for shorter sentences, purposeful transitions, and a concluding paragraph that does more than condemn the system: propose an alternative (test-optional policies, portfolio-based admissions, strengthened GPA/context review, free universal prep, or replacing the SAT with school-based assessments). If you do those things, your argument will feel less like a complaint and more like a persuasive case a reader can’t easily dismiss.

Your tone is passionate and energetic, which can be persuasive—but at the moment it also reads as overheated and sometimes dismissive, which may make skeptical readers tune out. Phrases like “money grab,” “monopoly,” “no accountability,” “undeniable fact,” and “no student truly likes the SAT” are absolute claims; if you can’t prove them, they weaken your credibility. The conversational lines (“heck give me enough money…”) create voice, but they also risk sounding like a rant instead of an academic argument. You’ll sound more in control if you replace sarcasm and certainty with measured, supportable wording (e.g., “test-prep services can significantly raise scores, creating an uneven playing field,” and “many students report…”). Also consider acknowledging the best pro-SAT argument more fairly (standardization across schools) before explaining why it still falls short—doing that makes your rebuttal feel stronger, not weaker.

For improvement, focus on structure and support. Break the draft into paragraphs, cut run-on sentences, and define key terms (“redundant,” “equalized measure,” “capacity and merit”) so readers know exactly what you mean. Most importantly, add evidence: data on the relationship between SAT scores and family income, research on how much coaching can affect scores, and studies on whether SAT scores predict college performance beyond GPA. If you want to argue for “removal,” address the current reality of test-optional and test-blind policies—why are those not enough, and what should replace the SAT (GPA + course rigor, portfolios, interviews, free statewide exams, etc.)? End with a conclusion that proposes a concrete alternative rather than mainly expressing dislike for the testing organizations; that shift will make your final position sound policy-focused and persuasive.

You have a clear, forceful argument: the SAT is redundant, financially driven, and inequitable because it primarily measures test-prep access rather than academic ability. Your main claims (redundancy with GPA, advantage for wealthier students, narrow skill focus, and lack of accountability) fit together logically, and you anticipate a common counterargument (“equal baseline”) well. What’s missing right now is a sharper thesis and a tighter line of reasoning that shows *how* each point supports your central conclusion. For instance, you assert that high school grades are already “verified,” so the SAT adds nothing—this is interesting, but you’ll need to explain what “verified” means (accreditation? course rigor? transcripts?) and why that verification should be enough for colleges across thousands of schools with different grading practices.

Your tone is passionate and engaging, but it sometimes crosses into sweeping statements and loaded language that can make readers skeptical. Phrases like “undeniable fact that no student truly likes the SAT,” “money grab,” “monopoly,” and “little to no respect” communicate strong feeling, but they also sound more like a rant than a college argument unless you support them with evidence and qualify claims you can’t prove universally. Instead of assuming the reader already agrees, aim for credibility: acknowledge that some students do benefit from standardized tests (for example, students from under-resourced schools who earn a strong score to counteract grade inflation elsewhere), then explain why you still think the harms outweigh the benefits. For revision, focus first on organization and sentence control. Right now, most of the draft is one long run-on sentence; breaking it into paragraphs with topic sentences will immediately make your argument easier to follow. Consider a structure like: (1) thesis + why colleges use the SAT, (2) equity/resource argument with specific examples or data, (3) redundancy/limited skill measurement, (4) what should replace it (test-optional? improved holistic review? stronger transcript evaluation? free prep programs?). To strengthen the argument, though, you’ll want to turn these points into a more structured case with a thesis that previews your main reasons, then develop each reason in its own paragraph with specific support. Your tone is passionate and forceful, which can be effective, but in academic argument it can also come across as venting—especially with phrases like “money grab,” “monopoly with no accountability,” “undeniable fact,” and “no student truly likes the SAT.” Those absolute statements invite readers to challenge you on exceptions rather than engage your main point. You’ll sound more credible if you qualify claims (“many students,” “often,” “research suggests”) and if you replace rhetorical flourishes (“heck give me enough money…”) with concrete evidence (costs of tutoring, data on score gains from prep, correlations between income and SAT performance). For revision, focus on clarity and control. Your opening sentence is attention-grabbing, but it’s overloaded and reads as a run-on; break it into shorter sentences and define what you mean by “redundant” early. Add a thesis near the start (one sentence that states your position and your 2–3 main reasons). Then build body paragraphs: one on redundancy/grade verification, one on socioeconomic bias and access to prep, one on limited skill coverage and how that disadvantages students with strengths outside SAT-tested areas. Your tone is passionate, which can work in an argumentative piece, but some of your phrasing risks sounding rant-like or absolute in a way that invites readers to dismiss you. For improvement, focus on clarity and mechanics so your strongest points don’t get lost. Several sentences are run-ons with multiple claims connected by commas; break them into shorter units, and use transitions to show logical steps (Because/However/For example/As a result). Define key terms (what exactly do you mean by “redundant”? redundant for admissions decisions, for measuring readiness, or for curriculum alignment?). Watch for informal phrasing (“heck,” “money grab,” “like as if”) and proofreading issues (“infact,” “after the fact that”), and be careful with accuracy—high school grades aren’t universally “verified,” and “monopoly” has a specific meaning you’d need to substantiate. Your next draft will improve a lot if you (1) write a focused thesis, (2) build 2–3 body paragraphs with evidence, and (3) revise for sentence control and a more credible, academic tone.

You have a clear, forceful argument: you’re claiming the SAT is redundant (because high school grades already indicate readiness), inequitable (because wealth buys preparation), and educationally narrow (because it privileges English/math and ignores other strengths). That’s a workable thesis, and your draft communicates real urgency. Your strongest lane is the equity point—turn that into a focused core claim and back it up with a few concrete sources (score gaps by income, effects of paid test prep, changes under test-optional policies, research on predictive validity).
